coredata.dc:title: Role of carbon quantum dots in titania based photoelectrodes: Upconversion or others?
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Zhou, Jing
Chen, Jiangchun
Zheng, Jinlong
Yan, Bingyi
Zhou, Yan
Hou, Bingdong
Chen, Buyang
coredata.dc:description: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were synthesized by a facile and green pyrolysis method. The as-prepared CQDs show wide light absorption, tunable downconversion photoluminescence (PL) properties and excellent “upconversion” luminescence properties. CQDs were used to design the TiO2/CdS/C photoelectrodes for photoelectrochemical hydrogen evolution. The role of the CQDs was investigated based on the TiO2/CdS/C photoelectrodes. The results reveal that CQDs can enhance light-harvesting efficiency and improve their charge separation and transfer properties when coupled with conventional QD-sensitized photoelectrodes. Nevertheless, the contribution of “upconversion” luminescence of CQDs to the enhanced photoelectrochemical performance is negligible.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Photoelectrochemistry
Upconversion
Synergistic effect
Hydrogen evolution

xocs:hub-sec.xocs:hub-sec-title: Energy Conversion and Storage and Environmental Technologies

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors acknowledge the funding support by National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 51772049 ), the National Postdoctoral Program for Innovative Talents ( BX201600007 ) and the Project funded by China Postdoctoral Science Foundation ( 2016M600894 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) AFM image, (b) TEM image, and (c) UV–vis absorption spectrum and normal PL spectrum excited at the wavelength of 350 nm of the as-prepared CQDs. (d) SPP PL and (e) TPP PL spectra of as-prepared CQDs under 400 nm and 800 nm diode laser excitation, respectively, with various excitation intensities. (f) Log-log plots of the PL signals as a function of the excitation intensity under 800 nm excitation, while the inset represents a 400 nm excitation.

(a) A schematic showing the design and fabrication processes of TiO2/CdS/C photoanodes. (b) SEM image and (c) EDS spectrum of the as-prepared TiO2/CdS/C films. (d) Diffuse reflectance absorption spectra of the as-prepared TiO2, TiO2/C, TiO2/CdS, TiO2/CdS/C films.

(a) I-V curves of bare TiO2, TiO2/C, TiO2/CdS, and TiO2/CdS/C photoelectrodes under simulated sunlight at an illumination intensity of 100 mW∙cm−2. (b) Amperometric I-t curves at an applied potential of 0 V vs. Ag/AgCl at 100 mW∙cm−2 illumination with 25 s light on/off cycles. (c) IPCE spectra of bare TiO2, TiO2/C, TiO2/CdS, and TiO2/CdS/C photoelectrodes carried out at zero bias (0 V vs Ag/AgCl) with wavelengths ranging from 350 nm to 800 nm. (d) I-V curves of TiO2/C and TiO2/CdS/C photoelectrodes under the 800 nm laser excitation with the illumination intensity of 800 mW∙cm−2.

(a) EIS spectra under 100 mW∙cm−2 illumination for bare TiO2, TiO2/C, TiO2/CdS, and TiO2/CdS/C photoelectrodes. (b) Differential transmission spectra in the visible range for the CdS-QDs on a quartz substrate at a probe delay time of 1 ps, following pump excitation at 400 nm with a pump fluence of ∼10 μJ∙cm−2. (c) A comparison of the differential transmission transients of the CdS, TiO2/CdS, and TiO2/CdS/C films on quartz substrates at 510 nm wavelength following 10 μJ∙cm−2, 400 nm laser pulse excitation. Inset shows the decay transients in the first 100 ps.
A schematic illustrating the role of the CQDs in the photoelectrochemical hydrogen evolution.

head.ce:title: Role of carbon quantum dots in titania based photoelectrodes: Upconversion or others?

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry Engineering, Northeast Electric Power University, Jilin 132012, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry, Beihang University, Beijing 100191, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Center for Green Innovation, School of Mathematics and Physics, University of Science and Technology Beijing, Beijing 100083, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: University of Science and Technology Beijing

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: School of Chemistry, Beihang University, Beijing 100191, PR China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were synthesized by a facile and green pyrolysis method. The as-prepared CQDs show wide light absorption, tunable downconversion photoluminescence (PL) properties and excellent “upconversion” luminescence properties. CQDs were used to design the TiO2/CdS/C photoelectrodes for photoelectrochemical hydrogen evolution. The role of the CQDs was investigated based on the TiO2/CdS/C photoelectrodes. The results reveal that CQDs can enhance light-harvesting efficiency and improve their charge separation and transfer properties when coupled with conventional QD-sensitized photoelectrodes. Nevertheless, the contribution of “upconversion” luminescence of CQDs to the enhanced photoelectrochemical performance is negligible.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), as new members of the carbon nanomaterials family alongside carbon nanotubes, fullerenes and graphene, have drawn considerable attention in recent years [1–5]. They have been regarded as viable alternatives to the traditional semiconductor QDs due to their facile synthesis process, environmentally friendly properties, low biological toxicity, superior water solubility, and long term colloidal stability [6–9]. Furthermore, CQDs possess a wide light absorption range and strong and tunable photoluminescence (PL), triggering further research into their potential applications, especially in photoelectrolysis, energy conversion, optoelectronics, and medical diagnosis [9–14]. In addition to the typical downconversion PL, the CQDs have also exhibited excellent “upconversion” PL behavior under near-infrared (NIR) light excitation, enabling significant applications in the field of in vivo bioimaging and therapeutics due to their low background signal and phototoxicity [15–18]. Furthermore, it has been successfully demonstrated that CQDs could remarkably improve the photocatalytic degradation activity and the photoelectrochemical performance of semiconductors in the visible light region and that they could be employed as sensitizers when coupled with semiconductors for solar cells and related light energy conversion applications [5,6,14,19,20]. However, the fundamental enhancement mechanism of CQDs’ coupled semiconductor complex remains a source of intense debate and confusion [21–23]. There are some who attribute it to the “upconversion” luminescence behavior of CQDs, while others consider the low intensity light excitations used in normal photoelectrochemical experiments to be energetically incapable of exciting CQDs to emit “upconversion” luminescence [24–26].

Herein, we report the rational design of a composite film photoelectrode comprising CQDs, CdS-QDs and TiO2 nanocrystals, as well as the investigation into the role of CQDs in the photoelectrochemical hydrogen evolution. In this configuration, the matching of band edges between the CdS-QDs and the TiO2 nanocrystals results in the formation of a type II heterojunction that has been widely studied as a photoelectrode, while the CQDs, with their wide range of light absorption, can be regarded as electron-donor sensitizers when combined with the TiO2 semiconductor [27,28]. Theoretically, the “upconversion” PL property of the CQDs indicates their ability to be excited by high intensity NIR irradiation and to emit visible light, which may result in the excitation of the CdS-QDs and their subsequent injection of electrons into TiO2 (Fig. S1) [29,30]. Our results show that the TiO2 nanocrystals synergistically sensitized with CQDs/CdS-QDs exhibit excellent photoelectrochemical performance for hydrogen evolution. Although the as-prepared CQDs exhibit excellent “upconversion” PL behavior, the contribution of this “upconversion” luminescence to the enhanced photoelectrochemical performance is negligible, even under direct high intensity NIR-laser irradiation. The CQDs play two significant roles when coupled with conventional QD-sensitized photoelectrodes: enhancing the light harvesting efficiency and facilitating their charge separation and transfer behavior.

Herein, we report the rational design of a composite film photoelectrode comprising CQDs, CdS-QDs and TiO2 nanocrystals, as well as the investigation into the role of CQDs in the photoelectrochemical hydrogen evolution. In this configuration, the matching of band edges between the CdS-QDs and the TiO2 nanocrystals results in the formation of a type II heterojunction that has been widely studied as a photoelectrode, while the CQDs, with their wide range of light absorption, can be regarded as electron-donor sensitizers when combined with the TiO2 semiconductor [27,28]. Theoretically, the “upconversion” PL property of the CQDs indicates their ability to be excited by high intensity NIR irradiation and to emit visible light, which may result in the excitation of the CdS-QDs and their subsequent injection of electrons into TiO2 (Fig. S1 ) [29,30]. Our results show that the TiO2 nanocrystals synergistically sensitized with CQDs/CdS-QDs exhibit excellent photoelectrochemical performance for hydrogen evolution. Although the as-prepared CQDs exhibit excellent “upconversion” PL behavior, the contribution of this “upconversion” luminescence to the enhanced photoelectrochemical performance is negligible, even under direct high intensity NIR-laser irradiation. The CQDs play two significant roles when coupled with conventional QD-sensitized photoelectrodes: enhancing the light harvesting efficiency and facilitating their charge separation and transfer behavior.

Na2S, Cd(NO3)2, and glucose were purchased from Aladdin Company (Shanghai, China). Polyethylenimine (PEI) were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich. All reagents used in this study were reagent grade and were used without any further purification. Deionized (DI) water was used for all experiments.

TiO2 films on FTO substrates (F: SnO2, 14 Ω/square, Nippon Sheet Glass Group, Japan) were prepared according to the method in our previous work [31]. Briefly, a paste was obtained by mixing ethanol and P25 powder (Degussa) homogeneously (150 mg/mL). The TiO2 films were then prepared by spreading the P25 paste onto the FTO substrates with a glass rod using adhesive tapes as spacers and then heating at 450 °C for 30 min [32]. CdS-QDs were deposited onto the TiO2 films through the successive ionic-layer adsorption and reaction (SILAR) method. This involves the immersion of the TiO2 films on FTO substrates in a solution of Na2S (0.05 M) for 2 min, followed by rinsing with deionized water and immersion in a Cd(NO3)2 (0.05 M) solution for another 2 min, followed by another round of rinsing with deionized water. This immersion cycle was repeated 30 times until the desired deposition of CdS-QDs was achieved [33].

The CQDs passivated with PEI were prepared by a facile and green pyrolysis method. Typically, 2.0 g glucose and 0.2 g PEI with the average molecular weight of 25 k were added to 5 mL distilled water to form a clear solution, followed by heating at 150 °C for 20 min under ambient conditions, causing the clear solution to change to a dark yellow gel. After cooling to room temperature, the gel was dissolved and dialyzed against deionized water for 4 days. The CQD solution was then concentrated to approximately 1 mg/mL using a vacuum oven operated at 60 °C. The CdS-sensitized TiO2 films prepared as described above were immersed in the CQD solution and kept in the dark for 24 h for the fabrication of TiO2/CdS/C photoelectrodes. Bare TiO2 films were also immersed in the CQD solution as a control experiment. After sufficient adsorption, the photoelectrodes were removed and dried in ambient air.

Atomic force microscopy (AFM) images of the as-prepared CQDs were obtained using a Dimension 3100 with Nanoscope IIIa controller working in the tapping mode at ambient conditions. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) investigations of the as-prepared CQDs were carried out with a JEOL JEM-2100F microscope. The morphology of the TiO2/CdS/C hybrid films was studied using a field-emission gun scanning electron microscope (SEM, JSM-7600F from JEOL). SEM equipped with energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) was used to analyze the composition of the structure. X-ray diffraction (XRD) spectra of the samples were recorded using a Bruker-AXS X-ray diffractometer with Cu Kα radiation (λ = 1.5418 Å). The UV–vis absorption spectrum of the CQDs dispersed in water was tested with a Shimadzu UV-2550 spectrometer, and diffuse reflectance absorption spectra of the bare TiO2 films and the TiO2 films deposited with CdS-QDs and CQDs were recorded in the range from 300 to 800 nm using the same spectrometer with BaSO4 as the reference. The standard PL spectra were measured using a Shimadzu RF 5301 Spectrofluorophotometer.

Studies of the single-photon and two-photon pumped PL properties of the as-prepared CQDs were performed with 400 and 800 nm laser pulses, respectively. These 400 and 800 nm, 150 fs laser pulses were generated by a Coherent TOPAS-C optical parametric amplifier, which was pumped using a 1 kHz Coherent Legend™ regenerative amplifier seeded by an 80 MHz Coherent Vitesse™ oscillator. The emission from the sample was collected by a pair of lenses and into an optical fiber, which was then detected by a charge-coupled device (Princeton Instruments, Pixis 400B).

The photocurrent intensity vs. potential (I-V curve) measurements were performed in a standard three-electrode configuration. The as-prepared TiO2-based photoelectrodes were used as the working electrode, a platinum wire was used as the counter electrode, and a saturated Ag/AgCl electrode was used as the reference electrode. A mixed solution of 0.25 M Na2S and 0.35 M Na2SO3 with the pH adjusted to ∼9.5 was used as the electrolyte. The working electrode was illuminated from the back within the area of approximately 0.2 cm2 with a solar-simulated light source (100 mW∙cm−2, MAX-303, Asahi Spectra, Japan). The photoelectrochemical response of the sample under irradiation was measured by an electrochemical analyzer (CHI 660D Instruments, USA). A photoelectrochemical stability test was carried out in the same configuration at zero bias voltage vs. the Ag/AgCl electrode under chopped light irradiation (light/dark cycles of 25 s). The I-V measurements were also carried out under an 800 nm laser irradiation, and the illumination intensity on the photoelectrodes was 800 mW∙cm−2.

Incident photon-to-current conversion efficiency (IPCE) measurements were performed using a 500 W Xenon lamp with a monochromator [33]. The photoelectrodes were illuminated from the back within an area of approximately 0.12 cm2 by incident light. The generated photocurrent signals were collected using a lock-in amplifier (Stanford instrument SR830 DSP) synchronized with a light chopper (Stanford instrument SR540). The monochromatic illuminating light intensity was approximately 15 μW∙cm−2 at 475 nm as estimated using a radiometer (Photo-electronic Instrument Co. IPAS). Electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS) were measured on a potentiostat (CHI 660D, CH Instruments) by applying a bias to the open-circuit voltage over the frequency range of 10−1 to 10−5 Hz with a 5-mV amplitude under 100 mW∙cm−2 illumination.

For femtosecond TAS, the laser source was a Coherent Legend™ regenerative amplifier (150 fs, 1 KHz, 800 nm) seeded by a Coherent Vittesse™ oscillator (100 fs, 80 MHz). The 800 nm wavelength laser pulses were from the regenerative amplifier’s output, while 400 nm pump pulses were frequency doubled with a BBO crystal. Broadband probe pulses (400–1300 nm) were generated by focusing a small portion (∼5 μJ) of the fundamental 800 nm laser pulses into a 2 mm-thick sapphire plate. The pump and probe pulses were cross-polarized to eliminate any contribution from coherent artifacts at early times. Pump-induced changes in the transmission (ΔT/T) of the probe beam were monitored using a standard monochromator/PMT configuration with lock-in detection. The pump beam was chopped at 83 Hz, which was used as the reference frequency for the lock-in amplifier.

The CQDs passivated with PEI were synthesized by the pyrolysis of glucose, which underwent two chemical reactions: polymerization and carbonization [34]. The size distribution of the as-prepared carbon product is found to vary from 1 nm to 5 nm, as estimated from their heights in the AFM images, as shown in Fig. 1 a. The TEM image (Fig. 1b) reveals that the as-prepared CQDs comprise uniform and monodispersed sphere particles with diameters smaller than 5 nm, and the high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image (Fig. S2b) of a single CQD clearly shows the lattice spacing of ∼0.21 nm, which is in good agreement with the (1 0 0) plane of graphitic carbon [35]. To characterize the elemental composition of the CQDs, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed. In Fig. S3(a), a typical XPS survey showed C, N, and O elements on the surface of the CQDs. The presence of N indicated the successful doping of N in the synthesis of CQDs. The N dopant can inject the electrons into carbon-based materials, and then change the electronic and transport properties because N has a comparable atomic size to and strong valence bonds with carbon atoms [13,35]. Based on the benefits of N doping in CQDs, it can be extrapolated that the introduction of N into CQDs will further enhance their photoelectrochemical performance [36]. A high-resolution XPS spectrum of C 1s (Fig. S3b) displayed four main components: CC or CC (284.8 eV), C-N (285.3 eV), CO (285.9 eV), and CO (287.3 eV) bonds [37,38]. The N 1s spectrum (Fig. S3c) could be deconvoluted into three peaks at approximately 398.6 eV, 399.3 eV, and 400.3 eV, corresponding to pyridinic N, pyrrolic N, and quaternary N, respectively [13]. The O 1s spectrum (Fig. S3d) exhibited three peaks at 530.8, 531.9 and 532.4 eV, which were assigned to the CO, CO, and COC groups, respectively [39]. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) was employed to determine the surface functional groups on the CQDs. As illustrated in Fig. S4, the broad absorption peaks centered at 3250 and 2835 cm−1 were assigned to the stretching vibrations of OH and CH, respectively [38]. The peaks at 1608, 1434, 1334, and 1029 1420 cm−1 were attributed to the CO, CN, CC, and COC groups, respectively [37,40]. These functional groups improved the hydrophilicity and stability of the CQDs in an aqueous system. Raman spectroscopy was also performed to investigate the structure of the CQDs. As shown in Fig. S5, a disordered (D) band at 1325 cm−1 and a crystalline (G) band at 1597 cm−1 were observed, and the intensity ratio of the D to the G band (ID/IG) was 0.90. It can be seen from the UV–vis absorption spectrum (Fig. 1c) that the CQDs display a weak absorption peak at 360 nm and a long absorption edge throughout the visible region, which is consistent with previous reports [34]. This indicates that the as-prepared CQDs possess potential applications in photoelectrolysis and light energy conversion [34]. The PL emission spectra (Fig. 1c, Fig. S6 and Table S1) demonstrate their excitation-dependent nature, and the PL peaks shift to longer wavelengths upon the increase of the excitation wavelengths from 300 to 500 nm, while the strongest peak is achieved at the excitation wavelength of 360 nm. Overall, the above results show that the CQDs with diameters smaller than 5 nm have been successfully prepared and that they possess wide light absorption and strong, tunable downconversion PL properties.

The CQDs passivated with PEI were synthesized by the pyrolysis of glucose, which underwent two chemical reactions: polymerization and carbonization [34]. The size distribution of the as-prepared carbon product is found to vary from 1 nm to 5 nm, as estimated from their heights in the AFM images, as shown in Fig. 1 a. The TEM image (Fig. 1b) reveals that the as-prepared CQDs comprise uniform and monodispersed sphere particles with diameters smaller than 5 nm, and the high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image (Fig. S2b) of a single CQD clearly shows the lattice spacing of ∼0.21 nm, which is in good agreement with the (1 0 0) plane of graphitic carbon [35]. To characterize the elemental composition of the CQDs, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed. In Fig. S3(a), a typical XPS survey showed C, N, and O elements on the surface of the CQDs. The presence of N indicated the successful doping of N in the synthesis of CQDs. The N dopant can inject the electrons into carbon-based materials, and then change the electronic and transport properties because N has a comparable atomic size to and strong valence bonds with carbon atoms [13,35]. Based on the benefits of N doping in CQDs, it can be extrapolated that the introduction of N into CQDs will further enhance their photoelectrochemical performance [36]. A high-resolution XPS spectrum of C 1s (Fig. S3b) displayed four main components: CC or CC (284.8 eV), C-N (285.3 eV), CO (285.9 eV), and CO (287.3 eV) bonds [37,38]. The N 1s spectrum (Fig. S3c) could be deconvoluted into three peaks at approximately 398.6 eV, 399.3 eV, and 400.3 eV, corresponding to pyridinic N, pyrrolic N, and quaternary N, respectively [13]. The O 1s spectrum (Fig. S3d) exhibited three peaks at 530.8, 531.9 and 532.4 eV, which were assigned to the CO, CO, and COC groups, respectively [39]. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) was employed to determine the surface functional groups on the CQDs. As illustrated in Fig. S4, the broad absorption peaks centered at 3250 and 2835 cm−1 were assigned to the stretching vibrations of OH and CH, respectively [38]. The peaks at 1608, 1434, 1334, and 1029 1420 cm−1 were attributed to the CO, CN, CC, and COC groups, respectively [37,40]. These functional groups improved the hydrophilicity and stability of the CQDs in an aqueous system. Raman spectroscopy was also performed to investigate the structure of the CQDs. As shown in Fig. S5, a disordered (D) band at 1325 cm−1 and a crystalline (G) band at 1597 cm−1 were observed, and the intensity ratio of the D to the G band (ID/IG) was 0.90. It can be seen from the UV–vis absorption spectrum (Fig. 1c) that the CQDs display a weak absorption peak at 360 nm and a long absorption edge throughout the visible region, which is consistent with previous reports [34]. This indicates that the as-prepared CQDs possess potential applications in photoelectrolysis and light energy conversion [34]. The PL emission spectra (Fig. 1c, Fig. S6 and Table S1) demonstrate their excitation-dependent nature, and the PL peaks shift to longer wavelengths upon the increase of the excitation wavelengths from 300 to 500 nm, while the strongest peak is achieved at the excitation wavelength of 360 nm. Overall, the above results show that the CQDs with diameters smaller than 5 nm have been successfully prepared and that they possess wide light absorption and strong, tunable downconversion PL properties.

Furthermore, the pump power dependent PL was used to confirm that the as-prepared CQDs exhibit “upconversion” luminescence. Fig. 1d and e reveal the PL spectra of the as-prepared CQDs under 400 nm and 800 nm laser excitation, respectively, at various excitation intensities. It can be seen that the CQDs exhibit strong emissions in the visible region under both visible light (400 nm) and NIR (800 nm) excitation. Fig. 1f shows the logarithmic plots of the pump power dependent PL under 800 and 400 nm excitation. The inset of Fig. 1f highlights the linear dependence of the emission intensity on pump power, demonstrating that the PL behavior under 400 nm excitation occurred through one-photon excitation. Meanwhile, an obvious quadratic relationship is obtained under 800 nm excitation, signifying two-photon excitation. The single-photon pumped (SPP) and two-photon pumped (TPP) PL spectra show similar shapes and nearly identical peak wavelengths, indicating that the single-photon and two-photon excited PL behaviors of the as-prepared CQDs involve similar transitions. Thus, it can be concluded that the as-prepared CQDs do indeed exhibit excellent “upconversion” luminescence properties that are achieved by two-photon excitation.

To explore the role of CQDs in solar energy conversion, we fabricated a TiO2 photoelectrode coated with CQDs and CdS-QDs, as shown in Fig. 2 a. TiO2 films were first prepared by the doctor-blade technique, using a paste consisting of Degussa P25. CdS-QD deposition was then carried out using the successive ionic-layer adsorption and reaction (SILAR) method, followed by the adsorption of the CQDs on the composite films [31,33,41]. SEM images (Fig. 2b) demonstrate that the resultant TiO2/CdS-QDs/CQDs (TiO2/CdS/C) composite films comprise a porous structure composed of nanoparticles with a grain size ranging from 25 to 70 nm. Elemental mapping of the TiO2/CdS/C was also performed to study the elemental distribution. Fig. S7 shows that the Ti, O, Cd, S, C, and N elements were distributed homogeneously in the sample, indicating that the carbon-QDs, CdS-QDs, and P25 contacted each other facilely. XRD patterns of the as-prepared bare TiO2 films, TiO2 coated with CQDs (TiO2/C) films, TiO2 coated with CdS-QDs (TiO2/CdS) films, and TiO2/CdS/C composite films are shown in Fig. S8, and it can be seen that the CdS-QDs were successfully deposited on the TiO2 films. Note that the characteristic peaks of the CQDs could not been found, which may be due to their small size and relatively low quantity in the composite films. However, energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) confirms the presence of Ti, O, S, Cd, and C in the as-prepared films (Fig. 2c), implying that the CdS-CQDs and CQDs are successfully deposited onto the TiO2 films.

To explore the role of CQDs in solar energy conversion, we fabricated a TiO2 photoelectrode coated with CQDs and CdS-QDs, as shown in Fig. 2 a. TiO2 films were first prepared by the doctor-blade technique, using a paste consisting of Degussa P25. CdS-QD deposition was then carried out using the successive ionic-layer adsorption and reaction (SILAR) method, followed by the adsorption of the CQDs on the composite films [31,33,41]. SEM images (Fig. 2b) demonstrate that the resultant TiO2/CdS-QDs/CQDs (TiO2/CdS/C) composite films comprise a porous structure composed of nanoparticles with a grain size ranging from 25 to 70 nm. Elemental mapping of the TiO2/CdS/C was also performed to study the elemental distribution. Fig. S7 shows that the Ti, O, Cd, S, C, and N elements were distributed homogeneously in the sample, indicating that the carbon-QDs, CdS-QDs, and P25 contacted each other facilely. XRD patterns of the as-prepared bare TiO2 films, TiO2 coated with CQDs (TiO2/C) films, TiO2 coated with CdS-QDs (TiO2/CdS) films, and TiO2/CdS/C composite films are shown in Fig. S8, and it can be seen that the CdS-QDs were successfully deposited on the TiO2 films. Note that the characteristic peaks of the CQDs could not been found, which may be due to their small size and relatively low quantity in the composite films. However, energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) confirms the presence of Ti, O, S, Cd, and C in the as-prepared films (Fig. 2c), implying that the CdS-CQDs and CQDs are successfully deposited onto the TiO2 films.

Diffuse reflectance absorption spectra were used to investigate the light harvesting efficiency of the as-prepared photoelectrodes, as shown in Fig. 2d. Although the bare TiO2 films can only absorb ultraviolet light, the deposition of the CQDs enables the extension of the light absorption range to visible light. The TiO2/CdS films exhibit strong light absorption at the wavelengths below 550 nm because of the low band gap of CdS-QDs. Upon the adsorption of CQDs, the light absorbance intensity of the TiO2/CdS/C films exhibits a marked enhancement in the wavelength region below 500 nm compared to that of the TiO2/CdS films. Therefore, the CQDs indeed increase the light harvesting efficiency, although they do not extend the light absorption range. Such superior light harvesting efficiency of the TiO2/CdS/C films is expected to lead to enhanced performance in the photoelectrochemical hydrogen evolution.

To confirm this, current density vs. potential (I-V) measurements were conducted in a three-electrode cell containing a mixed solution of Na2S and Na2SO3 as the electrolyte for the as-prepared TiO2-based films under a solar-simulated light source (Fig. 3 a). It can be seen that the TiO2/C photoelectrodes show significantly higher photocurrent intensity relative to that of TiO2, suggesting that the CQDs may serve as a sensitizer to enhance the photoelectrochemical performance of TiO2 for hydrogen evolution. This is because electronic coupling between the pi states of graphite-based CQDs and the conduction band states of TiO2 induced a smaller hybrid band gap compared to that of pure TiO2. Similar phenomena have often been observed in other compositions based on TiO2 and carbon materials, which was attributed to the formation of a Ti-O-C bond, such as in the case of carbon-doped TiO2 composites [42–44]. Meanwhile, the photocurrent intensity and open-circuit photovoltage of the TiO2/CdS photoelectrodes are obviously higher than those of the bare TiO2 photoelectrodes, demonstrating a higher light harvesting efficiency and a shift in the Fermi level to a more negative potential. This is due to the coupling between TiO2 and CdS-QDs in the composite system [30,45]. With the additional deposition of the CQDs, the TiO2/CdS/C photoelectrodes display a further increase in the photocurrent intensity from 5.98 mA∙cm−2 to 8.15 mA∙cm−2 at the potential of 0 V vs. Ag/AgCl. In addition, the as-prepared composite photoanodes exhibit a fast photoresponse and good photostability in the current–time curve measurement (Fig. 3b). Thus, it has been confirmed that the TiO2/CdS/C photoelectrodes with superior light harvesting efficiency exhibit better photoelectrochemical performance than the other photoelectrodes. Then, the question is, what other effects of the CQDs are favorable for improving the efficiency of the photoelectrochemical hydrogen evolution? In particular, does the “upconversion” contribute to such enhancement because the as-prepared CQDs show “upconversion” luminescence properties?

First, the role of the CQDs in photoelectrochemical hydrogen evolution was elucidated through the measurement of the incident photon-to-current conversion efficiency (IPCE), which can demonstrate which excitation wavelengths contribute to the photoelectrochemical performance. As shown in Fig. 3c, compared to the bare TiO2 photoelectrodes, the TiO2/C photoelectrodes show photocurrent behavior from 400 nm to 550 nm, which is consistent with the diffuse reflectance absorption spectroscopy results discussed above. The TiO2/CdS photoelectrodes exhibit a broad IPCE peak in the visible light spectrum corresponding to the bandgap of nanocrystalline CdS. Furthermore, upon the adsorption of the CQDs, the photocurrent intensity of the TiO2/CdS/C photoelectrodes shows significant improvement in the region of the wavelengths below 550 nm. Nearly no photocurrent action is observed with the TiO2/CdS/C photoelectrodes at wavelengths longer than 550 nm. This means that the NIR light region does not contribute to the overall enhanced photoelectrochemical performance, and the “upconversion” behavior of the CQDs might not work under the normal Xenon lamp excitation.

To further clarify this, I-V measurements were conducted under the 800 nm laser irradiation. As seen in Fig. 3d, there is an overlap in the I-V curves of the TiO2/C photoelectrodes in the dark and under light illumination. This is reasonable since the TiO2 films can only absorb light with wavelengths shorter than 410 nm, thus the “upconversion” luminescence of the CQDs at 510 nm would not be absorbed by the TiO2. For the TiO2/CdS/C photoanodes, such “upconversion” PL emission of CQDs would be energetically capable of exciting the CdS-QDs and their subsequent injection of electrons into the conduction band of the TiO2 [29,30]. The I-V curves of the TiO2/CdS/C photoanodes in the dark and under light illumination also appear to be nearly overlapping (Fig. 3d). This strongly demonstrates that the “upconversion” effect of the CQDs does not contribute to the photoelectrochemical performance even under the high intensity laser excitation. Through these experiments, we now can exclude the role of the CQDs as “upconversion” contribution to the photoelectrochemical hydrogen evolution in our systems.

Then, we fundamentally address the charge transport and transfer properties that usually play important roles in light energy conversion [45]. Fig. 4 a shows the Nyquist plots of the bare TiO2, TiO2/C, TiO2/CdS, and TiO2/CdS/C photoelectrodes, which were obtained by applying an open-circuit voltage under 100 mW∙cm−2 illuminations. Interestingly, it was observed that the semicircle in the plot of the TiO2/CdS/C photoelectrodes had the shortest radius, indicating a decrease in the solid-state interface layer resistance and charge transfer resistance on the surface. As a result, the TiO2/CdS/C photoelectrodes possess superior charge separation and transfer properties, which helps to suppress charge recombination, thus resulting in a better photoelectrochemical performance.

Finally, the photoinduced charge transfer dynamics between the different constituents within the TiO2/CdS/C photoelectrodes were examined with femtosecond transient absorption spectroscopy (TAS), which is widely used for investigating the charge carrier dynamics between heterostructures through the monitoring of electron and hole filling kinetics at the lowest energy levels [46,47]. For the present study, 400 nm (3.1 eV) 150 fs laser pulses were used to excite the electrons predominantly from the CdS component. The pump fluence was kept to the minimal value of 10 μJ∙cm−2 per pulse in order to mimic a low-intensity regime comparable to that of regular photoelectrochemical operations and to avoid any second-order effects. For pure CdS-QDs (coated on quartz substrates), a dominant photobleaching (PB) band (i.e., ΔT/T > 0 over 465–580 nm spectra range) was observed (Fig. 4b), with the peak at 510 nm or 2.43 eV. Such PB signature in semiconductor QDs is mainly due to the state filling of the 1s electron level [46–48]. For comparison, the electron dynamics in the hybrid CdS samples (i.e., the TiO2/CdS and the TiO2/CdS/C) were also monitored at the probe wavelength of 510 nm. The resultant decay curves and multiexponential fits are shown in Fig. 4c. It can be clearly seen that lifetime shortening occurs when the CdS-QDs are hybridized with TiO2 and TiO2/C. Under the same experimental conditions, the differential transmittance signals observed in the bare TiO2, CQDs and TiO2/C films (of similar thickness to the TiO2/CdS/C films) are negligible. Therefore, this lifetime shortening can undeniably be assigned to the ultrafast electron transfer from the CdS conduction band to the TiO2 and TiO2/C following a subtraction of the CdS dynamics and a deconvolution procedure [7,49]. The transfer efficiencies (and intensity-weighted transfer lifetimes) for CdS hybridized with TiO2 and with TiO2/C were found to be 2% (50 ps) and 10% (360 ps), respectively. The increased efficiency in the latter can be understood as the result of the existence of more pathways, which led to an increase in the electron transfer rates. The overall efficiency of the electron transfer is still low, which may be attributed to the limited interface for CdS hybridization. The efficiency of the TiO2/CdS/C films in functional devices can be further improved by the application of a bias voltage, which can effectively decrease the probability of recombination of the photoinduced electron/hole pairs, resulting in high photoelectrochemical efficiency [50].

The CQDs displayed a wide light absorption as seen in the UV–vis absorption spectrum (Fig. 1c), a tunable downconversion PL property as seen in the PL emission spectra (Fig. 1c, Fig. S6 and Table S1) and excellent “upconversion” luminescence properties as seen from the SPP and TPP PL spectra (Fig. 1d and e). These results suggest that the CQDs could be employed as sensitizers for semiconductors to improve the photoelectrochemical performance in energy conversion applications. It has been confirmed that the TiO2/CdS/C photoelectrodes exhibit better photoelectrochemical hydrogen evolution performance than the other photoelectrodes (TiO2/CdS, TiO2/C and TiO2) (Fig. 3a and b). The CQDs were favorable for improving the efficiency of the photoelectrochemical performance. It was necessary to elucidate the enhancement mechanism and whether the “upconversion” luminescence behavior of the CQDs enhances the photoelectrochemical performance. The results of IPCE measurements for the TiO2/CdS/C photoelectrodes showed that no photocurrent appeared at the wavelengths larger than 550 nm (Fig. 3c). The I-V measurements under the 800 nm laser irradiation for the TiO2/CdS/C photoanodes showed the I-V curves in the dark and under light illumination were nearly overlapping (Fig. 3d). These experiments demonstrate that the role of the CQDs as the “upconversion” contribution to photoelectrochemical hydrogen evolution can be excluded in our systems. The Nyquist plots revealed the TiO2/CdS/C photoelectrodes exhibited superior charge separation and transfer compared to other photoelectrodes (Fig. 4a). The TAS showed lifetime shortening for TiO2/CdS/C, which can be assigned to the ultrafast electron transfer from the CdS conduction band to TiO2/C (Fig. 4c). Based on the abovementioned results, we can conclude that the role of the CQDs in photoelectrochemical hydrogen evolution mainly includes enhancing the light harvesting efficiency of conventional QD-sensitized photoelectrodes, as well as facilitating their charge separation and transfer behavior, as shown in Scheme 1 .

The CQDs displayed a wide light absorption as seen in the UV–vis absorption spectrum (Fig. 1c), a tunable downconversion PL property as seen in the PL emission spectra (Fig. 1c, Fig. S6 and Table S1) and excellent “upconversion” luminescence properties as seen from the SPP and TPP PL spectra (Fig. 1d and e). These results suggest that the CQDs could be employed as sensitizers for semiconductors to improve the photoelectrochemical performance in energy conversion applications. It has been confirmed that the TiO2/CdS/C photoelectrodes exhibit better photoelectrochemical hydrogen evolution performance than the other photoelectrodes (TiO2/CdS, TiO2/C and TiO2) (Fig. 3a and b). The CQDs were favorable for improving the efficiency of the photoelectrochemical performance. It was necessary to elucidate the enhancement mechanism and whether the “upconversion” luminescence behavior of the CQDs enhances the photoelectrochemical performance. The results of IPCE measurements for the TiO2/CdS/C photoelectrodes showed that no photocurrent appeared at the wavelengths larger than 550 nm (Fig. 3c). The I-V measurements under the 800 nm laser irradiation for the TiO2/CdS/C photoanodes showed the I-V curves in the dark and under light illumination were nearly overlapping (Fig. 3d). These experiments demonstrate that the role of the CQDs as the “upconversion” contribution to photoelectrochemical hydrogen evolution can be excluded in our systems. The Nyquist plots revealed the TiO2/CdS/C photoelectrodes exhibited superior charge separation and transfer compared to other photoelectrodes (Fig. 4a). The TAS showed lifetime shortening for TiO2/CdS/C, which can be assigned to the ultrafast electron transfer from the CdS conduction band to TiO2/C (Fig. 4c). Based on the abovementioned results, we can conclude that the role of the CQDs in photoelectrochemical hydrogen evolution mainly includes enhancing the light harvesting efficiency of conventional QD-sensitized photoelectrodes, as well as facilitating their charge separation and transfer behavior, as shown in Scheme 1 .

In summary, CQDs were synthesized and used to design the TiO2/CdS/C photoelectrodes for photoelectrochemical hydrogen evolution. The roles of the CQDs were investigated based on the TiO2/CdS/C photoelectrodes. The addition of the CQDs not only remarkably enhances the light harvesting efficiency compared to the conventional TiO2/CdS photoelectrodes but also contributes to the superior charge separation and transfer properties of the resultant TiO2/CdS/C photoelectrodes. Comparing our results with the literature, we find that the TiO2/CdS/C photoelectrode shows a much higher photocurrent intensity (8.15 mA∙cm−2 at the potential of 0 V vs. Ag/AgCl) than most of the other photocatalysts reported by Refs. [51,52]. The synergistic effect of the CQDs and CdS-QDs contributes to a significant enhancement of the overall efficiency. Investigation into the photoelectrochemical behavior of the TiO2/CdS/C films under the direct high intensity NIR-laser irradiation revealed that the “upconversion” luminescence of the CQDs provides a negligible contribution to the enhanced photoelectrochemical performance of the photoelectrodes. A general evaluation via a systematic approach is necessary to fundamentally understand the role of CQDs in photoelectrochemical hydrogen evolution, as well as other solar energy conversion and photocatalysis processes. This work provides a very promising approach for the development of photoanodes in the photoelectrochemical cell with enhanced power conversion efficiency.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge the funding support by National Natural Science Foundation of China (51772049), the National Postdoctoral Program for Innovative Talents (BX201600007) and the Project funded by China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2016M600894).
